title: Metrics, thresholds and baselines

publication.changeSummary: Source-linked AI Foundations draft for Metrics, thresholds and baselines; all claims, examples, metrics, code, safety boundaries, and answer oracles require independent human review. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: scikit-learn User Guide; 1.9.x target, accessed 2026-07; User Guide sections 3 and 5: metrics, scoring, cross-validation, inspection, uncertainty, and baselines; atomic topic: Metrics, thresholds and baselines
sources.claim: Evaluation chooses metrics, thresholds, baselines, slices, and uncertainty from the task and decision costs; a metric summarizes selected errors rather than establishing overall quality.

sources.claim: Evaluation chooses metrics, thresholds, baselines, slices, and uncertainty from the task and decision costs; a metric summarizes selected errors rather than establishing overall quality.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Evaluation chooses metrics, thresholds, baselines, slices, and uncertainty from the task and decision costs; a metric summarizes selected errors rather than establishing overall quality.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A rare-event classifier table includes prevalence, confusion counts, precision, recall, threshold, baseline, and bootstrap interval rather than accuracy alone. Your goal is to create and justify an evaluation table for regression, classification or generation. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Supervised learning; Generative AI, LLMs and retrieval; Classification and probabilities; Problem, target and success measure. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Bounded operational model
sections.body: Evaluation chooses metrics, thresholds, baselines, slices, and uncertainty from the task and decision costs; a metric summarizes selected errors rather than establishing overall quality. Any capability or quality claim remains limited to an explicit task, population, conditions, baseline, and recorded evidence.

sections.heading: Failure boundary and non-claim
sections.body: Metrics trade off, averages hide tails and groups, generated-output metrics are partial, threshold tuning can overfit, confidence intervals rely on sampling assumptions, and benchmark contamination inflates evidence. The learner must reject this overclaim: The model with the highest single benchmark score is best, and a metric improvement proves practical significance for every user.

includes: Choose task and decision metrics, compare simple baselines and report uncertainty rather than one headline score.
Create and justify an evaluation table for regression, classification or generation.
excludes: Metrics trade off, averages hide tails and groups, generated-output metrics are partial, threshold tuning can overfit, confidence intervals rely on sampling assumptions, and benchmark contamination inflates evidence.

outcomes: Create and justify an evaluation table for regression, classification or generation.

examples.title: Metrics, thresholds and baselines: bounded direct case
examples.situation: A rare-event classifier table includes prevalence, confusion counts, precision, recall, threshold, baseline, and bootstrap interval rather than accuracy alone.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the exact data, state, metric, or tool behavior: A rare-event classifier table includes prevalence, confusion counts, precision, recall, threshold, baseline, and bootstrap interval rather than accuracy alone.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Verify the claim boundary with: Predeclare decision-relevant metrics and baselines, report thresholds, uncertainty, slices, and failure examples, and limit claims to the evaluated population and conditions.
examples.result: This example supports only the atomic outcome because Predeclare decision-relevant metrics and baselines, report thresholds, uncertainty, slices, and failure examples, and limit claims to the evaluated population and conditions.

examples.title: Metrics, thresholds and baselines: stress and transfer case
examples.situation: A generation system has overlap and human groundedness results with disagreements reported, because neither measure alone captures correctness and usefulness.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name the changed population, input, assumption, or failure: A generation system has overlap and human groundedness results with disagreements reported, because neither measure alone captures correctness and usefulness.

Step 2 — Keep the non-claim visible: Metrics trade off, averages hide tails and groups, generated-output metrics are partial, threshold tuning can overfit, confidence intervals rely on sampling assumptions, and benchmark contamination inflates evidence.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting overclaim: The model with the highest single benchmark score is best, and a metric improvement proves practical significance for every user.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Create and justify an evaluation table for regression, classification or generation.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: The model with the highest single benchmark score is best, and a metric improvement proves practical significance for every user.
misconceptions.correction: Predeclare decision-relevant metrics and baselines, report thresholds, uncertainty, slices, and failure examples, and limit claims to the evaluated population and conditions.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Identify the first unsupported inference in this belief and the evidence required to narrow it: The model with the highest single benchmark score is best, and a metric improvement proves practical significance for every user.

analogy.example: an evidence and risk register linking metrics, stress cases, affected slices, accountable owners, controls, monitors, and stop decisions can introduce Metrics, thresholds and baselines only after its canonical technical definition and evidence boundary are stated.
usefulFor: evaluation, robustness, fairness, explanations, and lifecycle governance
limitations: no metric suite or framework checklist certifies safety, fairness, causality, or future robustness

Line 3: give the result and reject the common mistake.

recap.summary: You should now be able to create and justify an evaluation table for regression, classification or generation. Keep this boundary in view: Metrics trade off, averages hide tails and groups, generated-output metrics are partial, threshold tuning can overfit, confidence intervals rely on sampling assumptions, and benchmark contamination inflates evidence.
retrievalPrompts: Explain Metrics, thresholds and baselines with one technical mechanism, one evidence requirement, and one non-claim.
Why is this overclaim invalid: The model with the highest single benchmark score is best, and a metric improvement proves practical significance for every user.
Apply the boundary to: A generation system has overlap and human groundedness results with disagreements reported, because neither measure alone captures correctness and usefulness.
recap.nextReviewPrompt: Next: continue to “Robustness, drift and adversarial cases”. Before moving on, answer one retrieval prompt without reopening the explanation.
